title: End of module: Programming Paradigms: OOP and Functional

questions.text: Which special method is called automatically when a new object is created from a class, e.g. `BankAccount('Alex', 100)`?
choices: `__init__`
`__call__`
`__main__`
questions.explanation: `__init__` is the constructor: Python calls it automatically right after creating the new object, passing along the arguments given at creation (here `'Alex'` and `100`).

questions.text: In the `BankAccount` class, `_balance` is stored with a leading underscore rather than being freely writable from outside the class. Which OOP principle does this demonstrate?
choices: Inheritance
Polymorphism
Encapsulation
Recursion
questions.explanation: Encapsulation bundles data with the methods that operate on it and restricts direct outside access, favouring controlled access via methods like `deposit`/`withdraw` over freely modifying `_balance` directly.

questions.text: `class Cat(Animal):` makes `Cat` a subclass of `Animal`. What is the general term for a subclass extending a superclass and overriding one of its methods (like `speak`)?
questions.explanation: This is inheritance: `Cat` inherits `Animal`'s attributes/methods and overrides `speak` with its own version.

questions.text: Given `animals = [Animal('Generic'), Cat('Tom'), Dog('Rex'), Fish('Nemo')]` where `Cat`/`Dog` override `speak()` but `Fish` does not, what happens when the loop `for a in animals: print(a.speak())` runs?
choices: It fails, because `speak` isn't defined identically for every object
`Cat` and `Dog` each print their own overridden message, `Animal` and `Fish` print `Animal`'s generic message
Every object prints exactly the same message, since they are all in one list
Only `Animal`'s `speak` ever runs, because that's the type of the first list element
questions.explanation: This is polymorphism: the same call `a.speak()` behaves differently depending on each object's actual run-time class. `Cat`/`Dog` use their own overridden `speak`; `Fish` has no override so it falls back to `Animal`'s version (as does the plain `Animal` object).

questions.text: Which of the following are true of a pure function?
choices: Given the same arguments, it always returns the same result
It may modify a global variable as a side effect
It does not print, mutate its arguments, or write to a file
Calling it twice with the same input can give two different answers
questions.explanation: A pure function has no side effects (no printing/mutating/writing/modifying external state) and is deterministic — same input always gives the same output. Options 2 and 4 describe impure functions like `add_to_total`, which depends on and changes the external `total` variable.

questions.text: Using `def factorial(n): return 1 if n == 0 else n * factorial(n - 1)`, what is `factorial(5)`?
Using `def fibonacci(n): return n if n < 2 else fibonacci(n-1) + fibonacci(n-2)`, what is `fibonacci(7)`?
Every correct recursive function must have a base case. What is the base case for?
choices: It makes the function run faster
It solves the simplest version of the problem directly, without a further recursive call, stopping the recursion
It is optional if the function is short enough
It converts the recursive function into an iterative one
questions.explanation: The base case is the version of the problem solved directly (e.g. `factorial(0) = 1`) with no further recursive call — without one (or if it's unreachable), the recursion never terminates and eventually raises a `RecursionError`.

questions.text: Given `numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]`, what is `list(filter(lambda x: x % 2 == 0, numbers))`? Write it as a Python list, e.g. `[1, 2, 3]`.
questions.explanation: `filter` keeps only elements where the lambda returns `True`, i.e. `x % 2 == 0` (even numbers): $2, 4, 6$ from $[1,2,3,4,5,6]$, giving `[2, 4, 6]`.

questions.text: `map` and `filter` are described as higher-order functions. Why?
choices: They run faster than an equivalent `for` loop
They only work on numbers
They take another function as an argument and use it to decide what to do with each element
They must always return a new list, never an iterator
questions.explanation: A higher-order function takes a function as an argument, returns a function, or both. `map(function, iterable)` and `filter(function, iterable)` both take a function argument (here, a `lambda`) and apply it to each element, rather than the caller writing explicit loop mechanics.
